title: The Age of Innocence
author: Edith Wharton
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "The Age of Innocence" book by Edith Wharton?
Options:
A. She saw it (once her outfits were organized) just as a more prominent chance for strolling, horseback riding, swimming, and attempting the fascinating new round of lawn tennis; and when they at last got back to London (where they would go through a fortnight while he requested HIS outfits) she not, at this point concealed the energy with which she anticipated cruising. In London nothing intrigued her except for the theaters and the shops; and she discovered the theaters less energizing than the Paris bistros where, under the sprouting horse-chestnuts of the Champs Elysees, she had the novel experience of glancing down from the café overhang on a crowd of "cocottes," and having her significant other decipher to her however much of the tunes as he thought suitable for marriage ears.
B. She regarded it (once her clothes were ordered) as merely an enlarged opportunity for walking, riding, swimming, and trying her hand at the fascinating new game of lawn tennis; and when they finally got back to London (where they were to spend a fortnight while he ordered HIS clothes) she no longer concealed the eagerness with which she looked forward to sailing. In London nothing interested her but the theatres and the shops; and she found the theatres less exciting than the Paris cafes chantants where, under the blossoming horse-chestnuts of the Champs Elysees, she had had the novel experience of looking down from the restaurant terrace on an audience of "cocottes," and having her husband interpret to her as much of the songs as he thought suitable for bridal ears.
C. She viewed it (after her garments were organized) merely as a bigger prospect for walking, horseback riding, swimming, and attempting the intriguing new game of lawn tennis; and when they at long last got back to London (where they would spend a fortnight while he requested HIS garments) she never again hid the excitement with which she anticipated cruising. In London nothing intrigued her except the theaters and the shops; and she discovered the theaters less energizing than the Paris bistros where, under the sprouting horse-chestnuts of the Champs Elysees, she had the novel experience of looking down from the eatery porch on a group of "cocottes," and having her significant other decipher to her however much of the melodies as he thought appropriate for marriage ears.
D.
